refined_fr.refined_fr: Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.
refined_fr.rationale: The candidate FR bundles three distinct obligations: (1) terrain analysis, (2) obstacle identification, and (3) work-zone representation. Each is a separate functional concern with different data sources, processing logic, and stakeholder consumption patterns. The refined FR is solution-neutral (avoids 'mapping/modeling' terminology which implies implementation choice) and focuses on the core problem: making spatial features available in a structured, reusable form. Splitting into atomic sub-FRs allows independent DP selection and IM packaging for each concern.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Confirm that a worksite model exists in a machine-readable format (e.g., point cloud, mesh, semantic graph) that can be queried and consumed by downstream planning and execution functions. Verify that the model schema is documented and accessible to authorized stakeholders.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain elevation and surface properties across the worksite.
FR-NGHE-006.2: Identify and classify static obstacles (structures, vegetation, utilities) within the worksite boundary.
FR-NGHE-006.3: Define and maintain work-zone boundaries and access constraints.
FR-NGHE-006.4: Integrate terrain, obstacle, and work-zone data into a unified spatial model accessible to planning and execution functions.
refined_fr.note: The parent FR (refined) is now atomic and solution-neutral. The sub-FRs are also atomic and ready for independent DP and IM assignment. Each sub-FR addresses a distinct functional obligation and can be verified independently.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data via multi-modal sensing (vision, range, inertial) and fuse into a unified coordinate frame; store as a structured spatial graph with feature attributes (geometry, classification, safety zone) queryable by planning/execution functions.

DP2: Decompose worksite into discrete zones; assign feature capture responsibility to zone-specific acquisition modules; merge zone models via spatial registration and conflict resolution; expose unified model through a feature query interface.

DP3: Implement a real-time streaming pipeline that ingests raw sensor observations, applies incremental feature extraction and classification, and maintains a versioned spatial database; provide read-only snapshots and change notifications to downstream consumers.

DP4: Establish a declarative feature schema (geometry primitives, safety attributes, operational constraints); acquire raw spatial data; apply schema-driven extraction and validation; persist as a normalized model with audit trail; serve via standardized query API.

DP5: Delegate feature capture to autonomous agents (mobile platforms, drones) that operate under a shared spatial reference frame; aggregate observations into a consensus model via voting/confidence weighting; expose model with uncertainty quantification.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP4: Establish a declarative feature schema (geometry primitives, safety attributes, operational constraints); acquire raw spatial data; apply schema-driven extraction and validation; persist as a normalized model with audit trail; serve via standardized query API.

potential_sources: Schema evolution: changes to feature definitions may require re-processing historical data; mitigate via versioning and backward-compatibility rules.

Sensor-to-schema binding: if schema is tightly coupled to a specific sensor modality, switching sensors becomes costly; mitigate by defining schema in sensor-agnostic terms (e.g., 'obstacle' rather than 'LiDAR point cloud').
Planning/execution interface: if downstream functions assume a specific query API or data format, schema changes propagate; mitigate via API versioning and adapter layer.

Safety policy coupling: if schema embeds hard-coded safety rules (e.g., 'exclusion zone = 2m'), policy changes require schema redesign; mitigate by separating feature geometry from policy-driven interpretation.
coupling_check.notes: DP4 achieves loose coupling by separating concerns: schema definition (what features matter), acquisition (how to get data), extraction (mapping raw data to schema), and serving (how consumers access model). Decoupling is strongest when schema is defined independently of sensor type and safety policies are applied at query/interpretation time, not storage time.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: A worksite feature schema can be defined a priori (or iteratively refined) that covers the agreed set of site features and is stable enough to support planning/execution without frequent redesign.
ASSUMPTION: Raw spatial data (from sensors or manual input) can be reliably extracted and mapped to schema-defined features with acceptable latency and accuracy.
ASSUMPTION: Planning and execution functions can consume a machine-readable spatial model via a standardized query interface (e.g., spatial queries, feature filtering, attribute lookup).

RISK: If feature schema is incomplete or misaligned with actual worksite complexity, model will be insufficient for safe planning/execution; mitigation: iterative schema validation against real worksites and feedback loops.

RISK: If extraction/validation logic is brittle or sensor-dependent, model quality will degrade under varying conditions (lighting, occlusion, sensor drift); mitigation: robust feature detection, confidence scoring, and human-in-the-loop validation.

RISK: If audit trail and versioning are not enforced, model integrity and traceability for safety-critical decisions may be compromised; mitigation: immutable log of model changes, timestamps, and provenance.
RISK: If query API is not well-specified or evolves frequently, downstream consumers will face integration burden; mitigation: API contract testing, semantic versioning, and deprecation policy.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP4 to explicitly separate three sub-concerns: (1) Feature Schema Definition (declarative, policy-agnostic, versioned), (2) Spatial Data Acquisition & Extraction (sensor-agnostic, confidence-scored, with fallback/validation), and (3) Model Serving & Query Interface (standardized, versioned, with change notifications). This decomposition clarifies ownership boundaries (IM layer) and reduces coupling between schema evolution, sensor changes, and downstream consumer updates. Additionally, introduce an explicit Safety Policy Interpretation layer that applies worksite safety rules to the neutral spatial model at query time, rather than embedding policies in the schema itself.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized module responsible for declarative safety schema definition, spatial data acquisition and validation, normalized model persistence with audit trail, and standardized query API for safety-critical feature extraction and constraint enforcement.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound raw spatial data (geometry primitives, sensor readings, operational state) from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems for schema-driven extraction and validation.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Outbound safety constraint violations, audit trail events, and validated feature state queries to dependent consumers (Vehicle, Teleoperation, NGHE Solution).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional interface for operational constraint queries (inbound) and constraint-aware task feasibility responses (outbound).

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: schema definition, validation logic, audit trail persistence, and constraint enforcement are all safety-domain concerns. Query API naturally resides here. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry ingestion and alert/tasking interfaces cross boundary.

Poor fit: NGHE is a top-level orchestration/integration layer, not a domain-specific safety module. Placing schema, validation, and audit logic here would scatter safety concerns across the solution and create tight coupling to NGHE's lifecycle. Schema and persistence would diffuse into Vehicle and Teleoperation for local enforcement.

Weak fit: Vehicle is a physical/operational entity, not a safety governance layer. Centralizing schema and audit trail in Vehicle creates asymmetry (Teleoperation would need separate validation). Constraint queries would diffuse to Teleoperation and Safety Services anyway.

Poor fit: Teleoperation is a control/command interface, not a safety data governance module. Schema and audit trail belong to a neutral, reusable safety layer. Placing them here creates Vehicle-side validation duplication and couples safety policy to teleoperation availability.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the natural owner of declarative safety schema, validation, persistence, and query API because: (1) safety constraints and geometry primitives are domain-specific safety concerns; (2) audit trail is a safety/compliance requirement; (3) a centralized, neutral module avoids duplication and coupling between Vehicle and Teleoperation; (4) standardized query API is best served from a single authoritative source; (5) external interfaces (Telemetry in, SafetyAlert/Tasking out) align with safety's role as a cross-cutting governance layer.
containment_risks: Risk: If Safety Services lacks sufficient abstraction, it may become tightly coupled to specific vehicle geometry or teleoperation command formats, reducing reusability. Mitigation: Define schema in terms of generic geometry primitives and operational constraint categories, not vehicle-specific attributes.

Risk: High query volume from Vehicle and Teleoperation could create latency bottlenecks if Safety Services is not designed for concurrent access. Mitigation: Implement caching, read replicas, or event-driven notification patterns for frequently accessed constraints.
Risk: Audit trail persistence may grow unbounded, impacting query performance. Mitigation: Implement retention policies, archival strategies, and indexed queries by time range and entity.

Risk: If schema evolution is not carefully managed, changes could break dependent consumers. Mitigation: Version the schema, support multiple schema versions concurrently, and provide migration tooling.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Acceptance criteria require 'worksite model/map generated and available to planning/execution functions for agreed set of site features.' Safety Services IM boundary directly satisfies this via: (1) declarative schema defining agreed feature set; (2) normalized model persistence as the authoritative worksite representation; (3) standardized query API enabling planning/execution access. Audit trail provides traceability for compliance with 'worksite safety plan and operational policies.' Verification is concrete: query API availability, model freshness, schema coverage against agreed features.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. FR maps unambiguously to Safety Services IM: schema definition (what features), acquisition/validation (data ingestion), persistence (model storage), and query API (availability to consumers). External interfaces (Telemetry in, SafetyAlert/Tasking out) establish explicit allocation boundaries. Ownership is unambiguous—Safety Services is the single authoritative source for worksite spatial feature representation.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP4 logically decomposes FR into coherent, implementable steps: (1) schema establishment (declarative definition of geometry primitives and constraints); (2) raw data acquisition (inbound telemetry); (3) schema-driven extraction and validation (transformation); (4) normalized persistence with audit trail (storage and compliance); (5) standardized query API (availability). Each step is necessary and sufficient. DP avoids over-specification (does not prescribe database technology, query language, or sensor types) while remaining concrete enough to guide design. DP is independent of IM choice—the logic holds regardless of which module owns it, though Safety Services is the optimal owner.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Schema definition and validation logic could diffuse if Safety Services lacks clear API contracts. Mitigation: Publish schema as versioned, language-agnostic specification (e.g., JSON Schema, OpenAPI); enforce schema validation at API boundary, not in caller code.

Risk: Audit trail persistence could diffuse if callers implement local logging instead of using Safety Services audit API. Mitigation: Make audit trail a mandatory, non-optional output of all schema-driven operations; provide audit query API with filtering by entity, timestamp, and event type.

Risk: Query API could fragment if Vehicle and Teleoperation implement local caching or shadow models instead of calling Safety Services. Mitigation: Design query API for low-latency access (caching, read replicas, event subscriptions); document SLA; monitor query performance and adjust architecture if latency becomes prohibitive.

Risk: Constraint enforcement logic could scatter if Vehicle or Teleoperation implement local constraint checks instead of querying Safety Services. Mitigation: Establish Tasking interface as the authoritative constraint-checking path; document that local checks are advisory only; audit all constraint decisions through Safety Services.

Risk: Schema evolution could break consumers if versioning is not enforced. Mitigation: Implement schema versioning in query API; support multiple schema versions concurrently; provide deprecation timeline and migration tooling.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). Safety Services is the natural, domain-aligned owner of all DP4 components: schema definition, validation, persistence, and query API are all safety governance concerns. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry ingestion (inbound interface) and alert/tasking responses (outbound interfaces) cross the boundary. No duplication risk—centralizing in Safety Services avoids Vehicle/Teleoperation validation asymmetry. Neutral ownership (not tied to Vehicle or Teleoperation lifecycle) enables reusability and decoupling. Recommended: Enforce API contracts and versioning to prevent schema/validation logic from diffusing into caller code.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Formalize Safety Services query API specification (e.g., OpenAPI 3.0 or gRPC proto) with explicit schema versioning, error handling, and SLA (latency, availability). Publish as contract to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and NGHE Solution.

Revision 2: Define audit trail API as a mandatory output channel for all schema-driven operations. Specify audit event schema (entity ID, timestamp, operation type, schema version, validation result, constraint violations). Implement indexed persistence (by entity, timestamp, event type) to support compliance queries.

Revision 3: Establish Tasking interface as the authoritative constraint-checking path. Document that Vehicle and Teleoperation must query Safety Services for constraint feasibility before executing tasks; local checks are advisory only. Implement audit logging for all constraint decisions.

Revision 4: Implement schema versioning and concurrent multi-version support in Safety Services. Define deprecation timeline (e.g., 2 major versions back supported). Provide schema migration tooling and documentation for consumers upgrading to new schema versions.

Revision 5: Design query API for high-concurrency access: implement caching (with TTL), read replicas, or event-driven notification patterns (e.g., publish constraint changes to subscribers). Monitor query latency and adjust architecture if Vehicle/Teleoperation experience unacceptable delays.

Revision 6: Implement audit trail retention and archival policies to prevent unbounded growth. Define retention period (e.g., 1 year active, 7 years archived), implement indexed queries by time range, and document archival access procedures for compliance audits.

Revision 7: Define geometry primitive and operational constraint categories in generic, vehicle-agnostic terms (e.g., 'obstacle', 'no-go zone', 'slope limit') rather than vehicle-specific attributes. This ensures Safety Services schema is reusable across different vehicle types and operational contexts.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence and excellent containment (0.92 score). However, the recommended revisions reveal critical specification gaps at the DP and IM levels that must be resolved before proceeding to sub-FR decomposition. Specifically: (1) Safety Services query API lacks formal specification (OpenAPI/gRPC contract), versioning strategy, and SLA—essential for preventing schema/validation logic diffusion into Vehicle and Teleoperation; (2) Audit trail API is not formally defined as a mandatory output channel with explicit event schema and indexed persistence; (3) Constraint-checking authority (Tasking interface) lacks explicit documentation of the authoritative path and audit logging requirements; (4) Schema versioning and concurrent multi-version support are not yet designed; (5) Query API performance and caching strategy are not specified, creating risk of Vehicle/Teleoperation implementing shadow models if latency is unacceptable. These gaps are architectural, not functional—they affect how DP4 is realized in IM (Safety Services) and how interfaces are contracted. Resolving them now prevents rework during detailed design and reduces diffusion risk. High safety criticality amplifies the need for explicit, auditable interface contracts before decomposing further.

revise.why: DP4 is logically sound but under-specified in three critical areas: (1) Query API specification—add explicit requirement for formal API contract (OpenAPI 3.0 or gRPC proto) with schema versioning, error handling, and latency SLA; (2) Audit trail specification—add explicit requirement for mandatory audit output channel with standardized event schema (entity ID, timestamp, operation type, schema version, validation result, constraint violations) and indexed persistence; (3) Constraint-checking authority—add explicit requirement that Tasking interface is the authoritative path for constraint feasibility checks, with audit logging for all constraint decisions. These additions refine DP4 from a logical decomposition into a specification that prevents diffusion and establishes clear ownership boundaries. Revised DP4 should read: 'Establish and maintain an authoritative, auditable worksite model by: (1) defining a declarative schema for agreed site features (geometry primitives, operational constraints) with versioning support; (2) acquiring raw telemetry data from Vehicle and external sensors; (3) extracting, validating, and normalizing data against schema; (4) persisting normalized model with mandatory audit trail (indexed by entity, timestamp, event type); (5) exposing standardized query API (formal contract, versioning, SLA) for planning/execution functions; (6) enforcing constraint feasibility checks through Tasking interface with audit logging; (7) managing schema evolution with concurrent multi-version support and deprecation timeline.'

assumptions: Safety Services has sufficient computational and storage capacity to support high-concurrency query access from Vehicle and Teleoperation without unacceptable latency.
Vehicle and Teleoperation can be contractually bound to use Safety Services query API exclusively for constraint feasibility checks (no local shadow models).
Audit trail retention and archival policies can be implemented within Safety Services without impacting query performance.
Schema versioning and concurrent multi-version support can be implemented without creating unmanageable complexity in Safety Services.
Telemetry inbound interface (from Vehicle and external sensors) can be standardized sufficiently to enable schema-driven extraction and validation.
open_risks: Query API latency could exceed acceptable thresholds for Vehicle real-time planning, forcing Vehicle to implement local caching or shadow models despite contractual prohibition. Mitigation: Design query API with explicit latency SLA (e.g., <100ms p99); implement caching, read replicas, or event-driven subscriptions; monitor and adjust architecture if SLA is violated.

Audit trail growth could become unbounded, impacting query performance and storage costs. Mitigation: Define retention policy (e.g., 1 year active, 7 years archived); implement indexed queries by time range; implement archival and purge procedures.

Schema evolution could break Vehicle and Teleoperation if versioning is not enforced strictly. Mitigation: Implement schema versioning in query API; support multiple versions concurrently; define deprecation timeline (e.g., 2 major versions back); provide migration tooling.

Constraint-checking authority could be undermined if Vehicle or Teleoperation implement local constraint checks and ignore Tasking interface. Mitigation: Establish Tasking as the authoritative path; audit all constraint decisions; document that local checks are advisory only; enforce through operational procedures and monitoring.

Safety Services could become a bottleneck if query API is not designed for high concurrency. Mitigation: Implement caching, read replicas, or event-driven notification patterns; monitor query latency and throughput; adjust architecture if bottleneck emerges.
evidence_needed: Formal specification of Safety Services query API (OpenAPI 3.0 or gRPC proto) with schema versioning, error handling, and latency SLA.
Formal specification of audit trail API with standardized event schema and indexed persistence requirements.
Performance analysis or prototype demonstrating that query API can meet latency SLA under expected Vehicle/Teleoperation query load.
Schema versioning strategy document defining concurrent multi-version support, deprecation timeline, and migration tooling.
Operational procedures document establishing Tasking interface as the authoritative constraint-checking path and audit logging requirements.
Audit trail retention and archival policy document defining retention periods, indexed query requirements, and archival access procedures.
Geometry primitive and operational constraint taxonomy document defining generic, vehicle-agnostic categories (e.g., 'obstacle', 'no-go zone', 'slope limit') to ensure schema reusability.
